mathml_representations_list:
<mstyle id="custom-group-4" class="custom-group"><mi id="mi_130">d</mi>
        <mo stretchy="false">(</mo>
        <mo>∂</mo>
        <mi mathvariant="normal" id="mi_131">Ω</mi>
        </mstyle>

concepts_list.name: u_field
concepts_list.justification: Appears multiple times as a bold vector variable in momentum and continuity equations, e.g., in the term ∇⋅u and u⋅∇u.

concepts_list.name: w_test
concepts_list.description: Test function used in the variational formulation
concepts_list.justification: Explicitly described as a test function in H^1(Ω) that vanishes at Dirichlet boundaries, used in the variational form of the momentum equation.

concepts_list.name: rho_density
concepts_list.justification: Appears as ρ in both momentum and continuity equations, and is used in the formulation of mass and momentum conservation.

concepts_list.name: b_source
concepts_list.justification: Appears as a bold vector in the momentum equation as ρb, representing external forces acting on the fluid.

concepts_list.name: p_pressure
concepts_list.justification: Appears in the variational form of the momentum equation as a term involving ∇⋅w and p∇⋅w, and is part of the stress tensor σ.

concepts_list.name: mu_viscosity
concepts_list.justification: Appears in the constitutive equation for stress tensor σ as μ(∇u + ∇u^T), indicating its role in modeling fluid viscosity.

concepts_list.name: T_traction
concepts_list.justification: Defined as T = n⋅σ and appears on the boundary ∂Ω, representing the force per unit area on the boundary.

concepts_list.name: Omega_domain
concepts_list.description: Main computational domain for the fluid system
concepts_list.justification: Repeatedly appears as Ω in integrals and domain descriptions, such as in ∫_Ω and Ω ⊂ R^d, indicating it is the primary spatial domain.

concepts_list.name: delta_Omega_boundary
concepts_list.justification: Appears as ∂Ω in boundary integrals and interface conditions, and is referenced in boundary conditions and jump operators.

concepts_list.name: d_Omega_volume_element
concepts_list.description: Volume differential element in the domain Ω
concepts_list.justification: Appears as 'dΩ' in multiple integrals over Ω, such as ∫_Ω, indicating it is the integration variable for volume elements.

concepts_list.name: d_delta_Omega_boundary_element
concepts_list.description: Surface differential element on the boundary ∂Ω
concepts_list.justification: Appears as 'd(∂Ω)' in the boundary integral term, indicating it is the integration variable for surface elements.

concepts_list.name: t_time
concepts_list.description: Time variable in the evolution of the fluid system
concepts_list.justification: Appears in time derivatives such as ∂u/∂t and ∂ρ/∂t, and is used in initial and boundary conditions.

new_concept_info.description:
Velocity field of the fluid
JUSTIFICATION:
Appears multiple times as a bold vector variable in momentum and continuity equations, e.g., in the term ∇⋅u and u⋅∇u.

Test function used in the variational formulation
JUSTIFICATION:
Explicitly described as a test function in H^1(Ω) that vanishes at Dirichlet boundaries, used in the variational form of the momentum equation.

Density of the fluid
JUSTIFICATION:
Appears as ρ in both momentum and continuity equations, and is used in the formulation of mass and momentum conservation.

Body force vector
JUSTIFICATION:
Appears as a bold vector in the momentum equation as ρb, representing external forces acting on the fluid.

Pressure field
JUSTIFICATION:
Appears in the variational form of the momentum equation as a term involving ∇⋅w and p∇⋅w, and is part of the stress tensor σ.

Dynamic viscosity of the fluid
JUSTIFICATION:
Appears in the constitutive equation for stress tensor σ as μ(∇u + ∇u^T), indicating its role in modeling fluid viscosity.

Traction vector on the boundary
JUSTIFICATION:
Defined as T = n⋅σ and appears on the boundary ∂Ω, representing the force per unit area on the boundary.

Main computational domain for the fluid system
JUSTIFICATION:
Repeatedly appears as Ω in integrals and domain descriptions, such as in ∫_Ω and Ω ⊂ R^d, indicating it is the primary spatial domain.

Volume differential element in the domain Ω
JUSTIFICATION:
Appears as 'dΩ' in multiple integrals over Ω, such as ∫_Ω, indicating it is the integration variable for volume elements.

Surface differential element on the boundary ∂Ω
JUSTIFICATION:
Appears as 'd(∂Ω)' in the boundary integral term, indicating it is the integration variable for surface elements.

Time variable in the evolution of the fluid system
JUSTIFICATION:
Appears in time derivatives such as ∂u/∂t and ∂ρ/∂t, and is used in initial and boundary conditions.
